title: Pyrography tip maintenance
subtitle: How to clean, recondition, and store pyrography tips for consistent results
excerpt: A pyrography tip that is clean, lightly polished, and stored correctly burns consistently and lasts for years. A dirty or oxidised tip drags, produces uneven burns, and contaminates the wood surface. This technique tutorial covers cleaning a hot tip, reconditioning a cold tip, polishing with fine abrasive, and correct storage.
sourceNotes: General pyrography technique from traditional decorative woodcraft traditions; tip care procedures from standard craft practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of decorating wood by burning a design into the surface with a heated metal tip.
The black carbon and metal oxide coating that builds up on a pyrography tip with use. Oxidation makes the tip drag rather than glide.
The interchangeable metal tip attached to the pyrography handpiece. Different shapes produce different marks: writing, shading, calligraphy, and ball stylus.

content.text: during use. A clean tip glides across the wood surface at low heat; a dirty tip requires higher heat settings that produce coarser, harder-to-control marks. Regular maintenance during and after each session is fast and extends tip life significantly.
: carbon transfer from the burned wood, and metal

from the tip alloy reacting with oxygen at working temperature. Carbon builds up quickly on tips that are too hot for the wood being burned. Oxidation accumulates more slowly and appears as a dull grey or black film on the tip face.

While the burner is on and the tip is at working temperature, wipe the tip face across a folded piece of fine-grit sandpaper (320) held on the cooling stand. Two or three wipes removes fresh carbon deposits before they harden. Do not wipe the tip on paper or cloth: the material will scorch and stick. The cooling stand is the correct surface for hot-tip cleaning.

After the burner is switched off and the tip has cooled, any remaining residue can be removed with a fine abrasive. Fold a piece of 320-grit sandpaper and rub the tip face in small circles. A leather strop charged with fine polishing compound can be used after sandpaper to bring the tip to a bright polish. A polished tip face gives the smoothest burn at the lowest heat.

A tip that has been stored for a long time may have a thick oxidation layer that does not come off with fine sandpaper. Work through progressively finer grades: 240-grit, then 320-grit, then the leather strop. The tip face should come back to a bright metal finish. If the tip is pitted (small craters rather than a smooth surface), the pitting is permanent; the tip will still work but may leave a textured mark in fine line work.

Store tips in a dry environment away from moisture. A small glass jar or the original tip box is ideal. Tips stored loose in a drawer contact each other and the fine points of writing tips can be bent or blunted. Bent tips can sometimes be reshaped cold by pressing against a smooth metal surface; heating the tip while reshaping is not recommended as it can anneal the alloy and soften the tip permanently.

At the end of each session: switch off the burner; wipe the warm (not hot) tip on the fine sandpaper once; allow to cool fully; store dry. This takes thirty seconds and keeps the tip in near-new condition indefinitely.
